reasoning: ACLED protest data for Croatia represents right-skewed, overdispersed event counts. Stochastically, any given 30-day period has a less-than-50% chance (typically 20-30%) of containing strictly MORE events than the 30-day average over the preceding 360-day period, as the mean is inflated by outlier months. This mathematical property strongly biases our baseline downward. For R1 (Nov 30), 23 of the 30 days have already passed without any notable publicized protests in Croatia, meaning the likelihood of accumulating enough events in the remaining 7 days to exceed the annual average is extremely low. R2 falls during the pre-holiday and early winter period (Nov-Dec), which traditionally suppresses protest frequency due to cold weather and holidays. R3 is the most distant, catching late winter (a period that saw inflation-related protests in early 2025), so it reverts closer to the statistical baseline but remains constrained by the overdispersion handicap. All probabilities are scaled according to the amount of elapsed time and expected seasonal activity, while reflecting increased horizon uncertainty.
